description: RLWRLD is a software company that develops foundation models through Artificial Intelligence and robotics.

build_patterns.name: Natural-Language-to-Code
evidence: "index.html을 자동 파싱하여 라벨/섹션 구조 추출 (하드코딩 매핑 없음)"
"코드값 → 사람이 읽을 수 있는 라벨 변환"
"섹션별 질문:답변 형태의 병합 텍스트 생성"
build_patterns.description: The sync process programmatically parses the front-end form (index.html) to extract labels and section structure, maps coded values to human-readable labels, and synthesizes section-wise question:answer text — an automatic transformation from UI text to structured/templated data (a non-ML but NL→structured-data pattern).

build_patterns.name: Continuous-learning Flywheels
evidence: "발송 로그: `email_logs` 테이블"
"모니터링: Resend 대시보드 + Supabase `email_logs`"
"sync/logs/ 동기화/백업 로그"
build_patterns.description: There are pipelines for collecting operational signals (email logs, sync logs, Notion sync history) and automated backups. While no explicit model retraining loop is described, the presence of structured logs and backups is a weak signal that the project could feed continuous improvement cycles or analytics.

build_patterns.name: Vertical Data Moats
evidence: "Supabase 전체 데이터를 `sync/backups/YYYY-MM-DD/waitlist.json`에 저장"
"가입자 데이터: email, full_name, organization, country, social_profile, form_data"
"Notion DB에 섹션 컬럼이 없으면 자동 생성"
build_patterns.description: The project accumulates structured, domain-specific user signup data and automates daily backups + schema evolution in Notion, which are basic ingredients for a domain-specific dataset that could serve as a vertical moat. However, there is no explicit model training on that data described.

discovered_patterns.pattern_name: Serverless event-driven transactional email pipeline + idempotency
evidence: Supabase Edge Function + Database Webhook + Resend API.
waitlist INSERT 즉시 자동 트리거 (프론트엔드 수정 불필요)
`confirmation_sent_at` 체크
발송 로그: `email_logs` 테이블
discovered_patterns.description: On row INSERT into the Supabase `waitlist` table a database webhook triggers a Supabase Edge Function (Deno/TypeScript). That Edge Function calls the Resend API to send a confirmation email. Idempotency / duplicate prevention is enforced by checking a `confirmation_sent_at` column and writing to an `email_logs` table with status (pending/sent/failed).
discovered_patterns.why_notable: This is a clean production pattern — serverless webhooks for immediate side-effects plus application-level idempotency — but it's commonly used in modern small-to-medium scale apps.

evidence: Supabase REST API → waitlist 테이블 INSERT
[즉시] Database Webhook → Edge Function → Resend API
[5분마다] sync-notion.js (cron)
[매일 00:00 KST] backup-supabase.js (cron)
Cron 설정: */5 * * * * /usr/bin/node /home/rlwrld/projects/waitlist/sync/sync-notion.js
discovered_patterns.description: Realtime / event-driven tasks (email) are implemented as serverless Edge Functions triggered by DB webhooks, while periodic tasks (Notion sync, Slack alerts, daily backups) are implemented as Node.js scripts executed on a host via cron. The architecture mixes cloud-hosted serverless functions with self-hosted scheduled processes.
discovered_patterns.why_notable: The explicit split of immediate serverless actions and cron-based sync/backup indicates pragmatic separation of concerns; this hybrid is practical for small teams but requires careful secrets handling (service role key used by cron jobs).

discovered_patterns.pattern_name: Schema-inferencing Notion sync via HTML parsing + auto column creation
evidence: index.html을 자동 파싱하여 라벨/섹션 구조 추출 (하드코딩 매핑 없음)
코드값 → 사람이 읽을 수 있는 라벨 변환
Notion DB에 섹션 컬럼이 없으면 자동 생성
sync/sync-notion.js — Supabase → Notion 동기화 + Slack 알림
discovered_patterns.description: A Node.js sync script parses the static form HTML to extract label/section structure (so no hardcoded field mappings are required). It transforms coded values into human-readable labels, composes section-based question:answer text, and programmatically creates Notion database columns if the target DB lacks them. Deduplication is enforced using the email field.
discovered_patterns.why_notable: Automatically deriving schema/labels from the frontend HTML and mutating the Notion schema reduces manual mapping and drift between form UI and downstream systems — a pragmatic automation that reduces maintenance overhead and schema mismatch risks.

evidence: 이메일 중복 방지 (Supabase UNIQUE INDEX)
Honeypot 안티스팸 + 3초 제출 쿨다운
중복 발송 방지 (`confirmation_sent_at` 체크)
discovered_patterns.description: Multiple layers of anti-abuse: frontend honeypot and 3s cooldown to reduce bot submissions, database-level UNIQUE index on email to prevent duplicates, and confirmation email dedupe logic using `confirmation_sent_at` to avoid multiple sends.
discovered_patterns.why_notable: Layered defenses are standard best-practices but their explicit combination (honeypot + cooldown + DB unique constraint + email send flag) shows careful thought for low-friction systems.

discovered_patterns.pattern_name: Automated E2E testing + large manual checklist
evidence: Playwright 기반 자동화 테스트 + 수동 체크리스트 226항목.
QA/run-all-tests.sh
QA/tests/ — Playwright 테스트 코드
Option_Checklist.md — 226항목 테스트 체크리스트
discovered_patterns.description: They maintain Playwright end-to-end tests and an extensive 226-item manual QA checklist. A script can run all Playwright tests to validate flows, supported by documentation of edge cases and options.
discovered_patterns.why_notable: High test coverage and a documented checklist indicate production readiness and discipline; standard for professional web apps but notable for a small project.

discovered_patterns.pattern_name: Daily full DB JSON backups with automated git commits
evidence: Supabase 전체 데이터를 `sync/backups/YYYY-MM-DD/waitlist.json`에 저장
자동 git commit + push
sync/backup-supabase.js — 매일 KST 00:00에 실행
discovered_patterns.description: A scheduled Node.js backup script dumps the Supabase data into JSON files, stores them under dated folders in the repo, and automatically commits & pushes them to git. Cron runs at KST midnight (UTC 15:00).
discovered_patterns.why_notable: Using git as a simple historical store for daily DB snapshots is pragmatic for small teams; it provides a human-readable audit trail but has limitations (size, security) once dataset grows.

discovered_patterns.pattern_name: Service role key usage for privileged cron syncs
evidence: sync-config.json contains `serviceRoleKey`: "YOUR_SUPABASE_SERVICE_ROLE_KEY"
Cron runs Node scripts that interact with Supabase using the service role key
discovered_patterns.description: Scheduled Node scripts use a Supabase service role key to access database contents for sync and backup tasks, indicating privileged server-side operations are performed outside of the Edge Function context.
discovered_patterns.why_notable: Standard approach for scheduled server-side operations, but it raises operational security considerations (key storage, rotation).

discovered_patterns.pattern_name: Absence of model orchestration / no LLM components
evidence: No mention of models, LLMs, embeddings, vector DBs, RAG, or inference in README content.
All integrations listed are: Supabase, Resend API, Notion API, Slack Webhook — no ML endpoints.
discovered_patterns.description: There is no evidence of any LLMs, compound model orchestration, or model routing. The system is event-driven and data-integration-focused rather than model-centric.
discovered_patterns.why_notable: Its absence is notable because the repository is from an entity named RLWRLD, but the project itself is a classic webapp/integration stack rather than an AI/ML system.

novel_approaches.approach: Automatic schema derivation for Notion by parsing the frontend form HTML
novel_approaches.why_novel: Parsing the static index.html to extract labels/sections and creating Notion DB columns automatically avoids manual mapping and reduces schema drift between UI and downstream storage.
novel_approaches.potential_impact: Reduces maintenance, accelerates form changes without manual downstream edits, enables more robust sync for evolving forms.

novel_approaches.approach: Event-driven, serverless email send with application-enforced idempotency
novel_approaches.why_novel: Combines Supabase DB webhooks + Edge Functions (Deno) + third-party Resend API with explicit DB-level flags (`confirmation_sent_at`) and `email_logs` for observability.
novel_approaches.potential_impact: Provides reliable, low-latency transactional email flows with clear audit trail and duplicate protection, suitable for small teams wanting serverless immediacy with robust logging.

novel_approaches.approach: Daily JSON DB snapshot stored in git with automated commit
novel_approaches.why_novel: Using git as a simple backed-up, human-readable time series for DB snapshots is pragmatic and easily browsable.
novel_approaches.potential_impact: Simplifies recovery and audit for small datasets; easy integration into existing workflows but may not scale to large data volumes or meet strong retention/security needs.

sub_vertical: Foundation models deployment and orchestration for developers
sub_sub_vertical: AI-powered automation tooling and lightweight edge integration for applications

signals: Comprehensive architecture and data pipeline documentation in README
Clear project structure and cron-based automation for Notion/backup
Edge Function (Deno/TypeScript) and Supabase integration described
QA tests and Playwright setup present

team_strengths: End-to-end implementation capability for a web-based launch list and related automation (form submission, email confirmation, Notion sync, Slack alerts, backups) as evidenced by the detailed repo structure and README
team_red_flags: Lack of publicly available founder bios or team pages; no LinkedIn references or multiple team members; reliance on a single author in public repos may indicate a small solo operation

evidence: waitlist-style launch list form to gather early adopters
highly technical, self-hosted architecture (Supabase, Edge Functions, cron jobs, Notion/Slack integrations)
no pricing page or sales-led process described; emphasis on self-serve setup and configuration files
docs and configuration hints (sync-config.json, Cron setup) imply developer-centric onboarding

revenue_model.monetization_approach: not described; appears to be open-source/self-hosted with potential paid features but no pricing mentioned

distribution_advantages: open-source style repository presence can foster community adoption
Notion/Slack integrations create ecosystem touchpoints and potential virality within knowledge/workflow teams
automation tooling (Edge Functions, cron jobs) showcases a repeatable deployment model

stage_evidence: Comprehensive architecture and data-flow diagrams describing end-to-end workflow
End-to-end feature set includes Launch List form, automated confirmation emails, Notion synchronization, Slack alerts, and daily backups
Edge functions (email) + cron-based integrations (Notion sync, backups) implemented in repository
QA folder with tests and edge-case documentation (Option_Checklist.md, Flow_Edge_Case.md, Option_Edge_Case.md)
Publicly documented setup and configuration (sync-config.json templates, Cron examples, Supabase setup guide)

core_features: Launch List form with duplicate check and anti-spam measures
Automatic confirmation emails via Supabase edge function and Resend API
Notion synchronization every 5 minutes with dynamic schema handling
Slack notifications for new signups on sync
Daily backups of Supabase data with git commit/push
differentiating_features: Automated Notion DB schema management (creates columns if missing)
End-to-end data pipeline from client form submission to Notion + Slack without frontend changes
Integrated backup workflow with versioned JSON backups stored in backups/ and automated Git operations
roadmap_signals: No explicit public roadmap in README; features are described as working with configuration templates and cron setup, implying ongoing maintenance
QA suite and Edge Function-based email flow suggest readiness for testing and iteration

integrations_mentioned: Supabase (REST API, database and edge functions)
Notion (database synchronization and automatic column creation)

use_cases.primary_use_case: Collect signups for a waitlist and automatically confirm via email while keeping Notion and Slack in sync and maintaining daily backups
secondary_use_cases: Real-time visibility into new signups via Slack alerts
Centralized archival of signup data for audits via daily backups
industry_focus: startups that require lightweight web-form to Notion/Slack integration with archival

product_risks: Dependence on multiple external services (Notion, Slack, Resend) increases failure points and potential outages
Data privacy and compliance considerations for storing form data in multiple services
Cron-based workflows require reliable server/host environment and correct time zones (KST/UTC alignment)
Edge Function and Supabase configuration could introduce vendor lock-in or migration costs
product_strengths: End-to-end, low-friction workflow from user submission to email confirmation, Notion syncing, and Slack alerts
Automated schema management in Notion reduces manual maintenance
Daily backup with versioned JSON and Git-based history for recoverability
Well-documented setup with templates and clear architecture

unique_findings: Single-source-of-truth UI parsing: the sync process parses the static index.html to extract label/section structures and question metadata (rather than maintaining a separate schema/mapping file). This lets the frontend be the canonical form definition and drives downstream automations (Notion schema, human-readable labels) without hardcoding mappings.

Automatic Notion schema mutation: the Notion sync will create missing database properties on the fly when it finds new sections/fields in form data, effectively performing schema migration of a third‑party product (Notion) based on the parsed frontend.

Event-driven confirmation pipeline with Edge functions: they use Supabase Database Webhooks to trigger a Deno/TypeScript Edge Function that calls Resend to issue transactional emails immediately, with DB-level dedup (confirmation_sent_at) and an email_logs table for observability.

Minimal-dependency server tooling: the Node sync scripts claim to run on Node 18+ without external packages, suggesting bespoke HTTP/JSON handling (Notion, Slack, Supabase) and deliberate avoidance of heavy deps for small utility processes.

Operationally-oriented backups-as-code: daily full-table JSON backups are git-committed and pushed automatically. This blends operational backup with code workflow (history, PRs), but introduces operational complexity around credentials, data growth, and repo hygiene.

High QA discipline surfaced in repo: Playwright automation plus an explicit 226-item manual checklist and edge-case docs indicates strong focus on repeatable QA and launch reliability uncommon in many early-stage product repos.

story_angles.headline: Serverless + Cron: How RLWRLD stitched realtime webhooks, edge functions and scheduled jobs into a resilient waitlist pipeline
story_angles.summary: RLWRLD mixes immediate event-driven pieces (Supabase DB webhook → Deno Edge Function → Resend transactional email) with lightweight scheduled jobs (Notion sync every 5 minutes, daily JSON backup + git push). The result is a small, pragmatic architecture that achieves near-real‑time UX, idempotent email delivery and operational observability without heavyweight orchestration.
evidence: waitlist INSERT triggers a Database Webhook → Edge Function (Deno/TypeScript) → Resend API to send confirmation emails
sync-notion.js runs every 5 minutes to parse index.html, infer schema and sync to Notion (no external packages required)
backup-supabase.js runs daily to dump full DB JSON and auto commit & push to git

story_angles.headline: Turning an HTML form into a schema: RLWRLD’s Notion sync that infers columns from the frontend
story_angles.summary: Instead of hardcoding mappings, their sync pipeline parses the static index.html to extract labels and sections, converts code values to human-readable labels, and auto-creates Notion columns. It’s a lightweight approach to keep product UX and downstream data model in sync without manual schema migrations.
evidence: sync-notion.js automatically parses index.html to extract label/section structure — “hardcoding mapping 없음”
form_data is captured as JSONB in Supabase while key fields are normalized in dedicated columns (email, full_name, organization, country)
Notion DB columns are auto-created if missing during sync

story_angles.headline: Betting against frameworks and heavy infra: why RLWRLD favors static HTML, simple cron jobs and no external Node packages
story_angles.summary: RLWRLD deliberately avoids a JS framework on the frontend, runs simple node scripts for integration tasks (with Node 18+ and no packages), and relies on small serverless functions + cron rather than a service mesh or complex orchestration. This minimalism reduces blast radius, dependency overhead and cost — but raises interesting questions about scale and maintenance.
evidence: index.html is static HTML/CSS/JS with no framework; instructions emphasize serving via simple HTTP server
sync-notion.js explicitly notes Node.js 18+ and that external packages are unnecessary
cron jobs are used for periodic tasks (*/5 * * * * for sync, daily backup at KST 00:00) instead of a managed workflow engine

story_angles.headline: A robotics-first codebase: RLWRLD’s repos show real expertise across EtherCAT, ROS2, cameras and pose estimation
story_angles.summary: Beyond the launch-list plumbing, the profile contains robotics-focused projects (EtherCAT hardware interface for ros2_control, Orbbec ROS2 wrapper, OpenArm URDFs, FoundationPose demos). This signals the team’s domain knowledge in control, perception and robot tooling — valuable context when evaluating product-market fit for robot-focused AI tooling.
evidence: Repository 'ethercat_driver_ros2' provides a Hardware Interface for EtherCAT + ros2_control
OrbbecSDK_ROS2 wrapper with device compatibility notes and a major v2 update
openarm_description and FoundationPose repos show investment in URDFs and state-of-the-art 6D pose estimation

anti_patterns.description: The project is primarily a composition of third‑party services (Supabase, Resend, Notion, Slack, git) with thin orchestration logic. Core value appears to be wiring and scheduling rather than proprietary functionality.
evidence: Uses Supabase for storage/triggers, Resend for email, Notion API for sync, Slack webhooks for alerts, and git for backups — majority of behaviour delegated to external services.
sync/sync-notion.js and backup-supabase.js are small scheduled scripts (Node.js) with no external package dependencies called out — indicates light orchestration layer.
README shows architecture diagram that is purely event-driven glue (DB webhook → edge function → third‑party API; cron → sync/backup).

anti_patterns.description: The codebase implements a focused feature set (waitlist, confirmation email, Notion sync, Slack alert, daily backup). This could be absorbed into larger platforms or implemented by integrators using existing SaaS tools.
evidence: Functionality maps closely to existing SaaS combinations (hosted forms + Zapier/Make + Mail provider + Notion/Slack integrations).
No evidence of unique ML/IP or proprietary algorithms — schema inference from HTML and simple mappings are described but likely replicable.
Public stats show a small audience (16 followers) and the product appears targeted and narrow (launch waitlist workflow).

anti_patterns.description: There is no clear, defensible technical moat: no unique data assets, models, or patented integrations. Replication risk is high since the solution heavily relies on off‑the‑shelf services and open standards.
evidence: No LLMs, models, or proprietary algorithms listed — Build Patterns indicate LLMs: [] and 'Absence of model orchestration'.
Core differentiators are automations and mappings (HTML parsing → Notion schema), which are brittle and easily reimplemented by competitors or internal teams.
Key components (email confirmations, backups, Notion sync) are standard integrations with many ready alternatives.

anti_patterns.description: The offering resembles many existing tool-chains and lacks a clear unique position in a crowded space of form/mail/CRM automation tools.
evidence: Features like conditional frontend fields, honeypot anti-spam, unique email constraint, and automated confirmation emails are standard in form/CRM products.
Notion sync and Slack alerts are common add‑ons provided by many integration platforms; no compelling unique UX or business model is documented.
Daily JSON backups with git commits is an operational convenience but not a market-differentiating feature.

anti_patterns.description: Operational and security practices raise real risks that are not addressed in the marketing/README: automated DB dumps to git, use of a service_role key in scheduled jobs, brittle HTML parsing for schema inference, and many external 404/errors observed in logs suggest the README may understate operational fragility.
evidence: sync-config.json requires a Supabase serviceRoleKey which, if stored improperly, grants broad privileges; README shows cron jobs that likely run with that key.
backup-supabase.js performs automatic 'git commit & push' of full DB JSON backups — storing PII/credentials in git repo risks data breach and compliance violations (GDPR/CCPA).
README mentions index.html auto-parsing to extract labels/sections; HTML parsing for schema inference is brittle and may break with frontend changes, causing Notion schema drift or data loss.
The content includes repeated 'HTTP Status: 404 (not found)' and 'You've reached the units usage limit' messages — signs of flaky external dependencies or insufficient error handling/observability.

competitors.name: NVIDIA (Isaac/Omniverse/Isaac ROS)
competitors.similarity: Provides robotics-focused foundation models, perception stacks, and ROS/robotics integration (simulation, pose estimation, GPU-accelerated inference).
competitors.how_different: RLWRLD appears to focus on tightly integrating foundation perception models with low-level hardware drivers (EtherCAT, Orbbec USB cameras) and ROS2-first open-source tooling and ops automation, rather than platform-scale simulation and GPU infrastructure that NVIDIA emphasizes.

competitors.name: Covariant
competitors.similarity: Builds AI systems for robotic automation that include perception, manipulation planning and production-grade deployment for warehouse/manufacturing use cases.
competitors.how_different: RLWRLD emphasizes reusable foundation perception models and developer-facing integrations (ROS2 drivers, SDK wrappers, open URDF descriptions) and appears more research/open-source-oriented versus Covariant’s vertically integrated product + service approach for specific automation tasks.

competitors.name: Open Robotics / ROS ecosystem
competitors.similarity: Delivers the core middleware, libraries and community standards (ROS/ROS2) used by robot developers that RLWRLD also targets and integrates with.
competitors.how_different: RLWRLD builds on top of ROS/ROS2 with opinionated, model-driven stacks (foundation perception models, EtherCAT hardware interfaces, camera SDK wrappers) plus ops/QA tooling and model assets — effectively delivering higher-level models and integrations rather than general-purpose middleware.

competitors.name: Roboflow / CV-focused platforms
competitors.similarity: Provides computer vision tooling, datasets, and model deployment for robotics and machine vision tasks (data + model lifecycle for perception).
competitors.how_different: RLWRLD emphasizes research-level foundation models for 6D pose and robotics perception (e.g., FoundationPose references), plus direct ROS2 and hardware driver integrations — combining high-end model research with robot-stack integration rather than pure dataset/model lifecycle tooling.

competitors.name: AWS RoboMaker / Cloud robotics providers
competitors.similarity: Offers cloud services, deployment and orchestration for robotics workloads and integration points for perception and fleet management.
competitors.how_different: RLWRLD appears to focus on on-robot integrations (EtherCAT, local ROS2 drivers), model-level research and developer OSS components, plus lightweight ops (Supabase + Edge functions + Notion/Slack sync), rather than full cloud-managed fleet services.

competitors.name: Academic / Research groups (e.g., NVlabs, academic CVPR teams)
competitors.similarity: Publishes state-of-the-art models for 6D pose estimation and perception used by robotics practitioners.
competitors.how_different: RLWRLD appears to commercialize and integrate such research-level models into practical robot stacks, drivers and operational tooling, bridging research models with deployment-ready ROS2/hardware interfaces.

differentiation.primary: Bridging foundation perception models with low-level robotics integrations and developer ops tooling — shipping research-grade perception (6D pose) together with ROS2 drivers, hardware SDK wrappers and deployment/QA practices.
differentiation.technical: Tight ROS2-first focus (drivers and wrappers: EtherCAT Hardware Interface, Orbbec SDK ROS2 wrapper, URDF/robot descriptions), use or integration of state-of-the-art perception foundation models (FoundationPose references / SOTA pose results), and pragmatic engineering: Edge Functions for email, Supabase-backed data tables, automated Notion syncing, Slack alerts, and Playwright QA pipelines.
differentiation.business: Open-source / developer-oriented GTM: providing reusable OSS components, SDKs and model integrations aimed at robotics developers and integrators, supported by ops/automation (backups, syncs, email pipelines) to lower integration friction; likely selling higher-tier models, support or cloud/enterprise services on top.
differentiation.positioning: Positioned as the practical bridge between cutting-edge perception research and deployable robot software stacks — an engineering-first foundation model vendor focused on robotics integrations, as opposed to pure cloud platform vendors or purely academic releases.

secret_sauce.core_advantage: Combining research-level foundation perception models (e.g., top results in 6D pose) with deep, practical ROS2/hardware integration and ops tooling — enabling fast uptake of SOTA perception on real robots through drivers, SDK wrappers and deployment workflows.
secret_sauce.defensibility: Medium. Defensible via accumulated model weights/datasets, tuned model implementations (SOTA pose results), and the engineering integrations (drivers, ROS2 packages, QA automation, ops scripts). However, large incumbents (NVIDIA, cloud providers) or well-funded startups could replicate parts of the stack; full replication requires both research excellence and robotics systems engineering experience.
evidence: "FoundationPose: Unified 6D Pose Estimation and Tracking... No. 1 on the world-wide BOP leaderboard (as of 2024/03) for model-based novel object pose estimation."

Repositories and READMEs for low-level robotics integrations: "ethercat_driver_ros2" (Hardware Interface for EtherCAT + ros2_control), "OrbbecSDK_ROS2" (camera SDK ROS2 wrapper), "openarm_description" (URDF/xacro files).

Operational and developer tooling in repos: Supabase waitlist with Edge Functions, Notion sync scripts, Slack alerts, daily backups and Playwright QA with a 226-item checklist — shows strong emphasis on production integration and developer UX.

competitive_analysis.moat_explanation: RLWRLD’s moat comes from the combined capabilities across three areas: (1) research-quality foundation perception models (demonstrated SOTA results), (2) robotics systems engineering (ROS2 drivers, EtherCAT integration, camera SDK wrappers), and (3) pragmatic developer/ops tooling that lowers integration friction. This combination is non-trivial to assemble: it requires both ML research and low-level robotics expertise. However, each element is individually copyable by large platform vendors or well-resourced startups, so the overall moat is medium rather than high.

evidence_quotes: FoundationPose: Unified 6D Pose Estimation and Tracking of Novel Objects
Strong generalizability is achieved via large-scale synthetic training, aided by a large language model (LLM), a novel transformer-based architecture, and contrastive learning formulation.
Automatic parsing of the actual front-end HTML (index.html) to derive form labels/sections and dynamically create Notion DB schema without hardcoded mappings.
Event-driven confirmation email pipeline: Supabase DB INSERT → Database Webhook → Edge Function (Deno/TypeScript) → Resend API, with dedupe via `confirmation_sent_at` and an `email_logs` audit table.
Operational data hygiene: daily JSON full-database backups stored in date-keyed folders plus automated git commit & push to preserve historical snapshots.
Notion sync that merges section-level Q&A into consolidated text fields and auto-creates missing Notion columns to keep downstream tooling in sync with evolving form schema.
Simple anti-abuse measures wired into the ingestion pipeline: honeypot field removal on submit and a client-side 3-second submit cooldown with UNIQUE index on email to prevent duplicates.
